item.title: How will the Fed respond to the Iran war’s fallout?
item.description: Market Questions is the FT’s guide to the week ahead

item.title: Lithium-ion battery blazes imperil supply chains, warns Los Angeles port chief
item.description: Demand for the storage device is expected to double by 2030 as geopolitical risks pile up

item.title: Oil prices in the driving seat as energy shock upends global markets
item.description: Investors are picking winners and losers based on economies’ exposure to inflationary surge

item.title: Iran war should prompt a North Sea rethink
item.description: Sweating the UK’s natural gas assets can support the country’s green transition

item.title: Iran war risks global food shock as fertiliser supplies cut
item.description: Growing number of fertiliser plants forced to shut, threatening rice and other harvests

item.title: Are markets being too complacent about the Iran war?
item.description: Sometimes crises do not blow over as investors might hope

item.title: Cold Hard Truths: Arctic Security in a Changing World
item.description: This week on War & Peace, Olga speaks with Gabriella Gricius, Senior Fellow at the Arctic Institute, to discuss Arctic security, Donald Trump’s ambitions to acquire Greenland, Russia’s and China’s interests in the High North, and how NATO and European capitals are responding.

item.title: Conflict and Consequences: The Global Impact of the New Middle East War
item.description: The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran is entering its third week, with no end yet in sight. Crisis Group experts offer a 360-degree view of its effects on peace, security and economic stability around the world.

item.title: Japanese Leader Wrestles with Middle East Shock in Planned Trip to Washington
item.description: Crisis Group expert Matt Wheeler assesses the importance of Sanae Takaichi’s forthcoming visit to the White House, amid war, tensions with China and U.S. policy shifts

item.title: Escalation or off-Ramp in the Gulf? And What Hope for a Ceasefire in Lebanon?
item.description: This week on Hold Your Fire!, Richard speaks with Crisis Group’s former President Robert Malley about the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, whether U.S. President Donald Trump will escalate or seek an off-ramp and what Iran might do. He also talks to Crisis Group’s Lebanon expert David Wood about the latest Israel-Hezbollah war.

item.title: Sudan’s devastating war rages on as regional rivalries deepen
item.description: Yet the Iran crisis may push Gulf rivals to set aside their differences and revive stalled diplomacy on Sudan.

item.title: BOJ seen waiting till April for rate hike amid Iran war turbulence
item.description: BOJ seen waiting till April for rate hike amid Iran war turbulence

item.title: Jet Fuel Prices Soar as War in Iran Ripples Through Global Aviation
item.description: As the war in Iran spills over into other parts of the Middle East, energy experts expect the price of several oil and gas products to soar over the coming months, driven by shortages. This will likely affect flight prices, with several airlines warning of anticipated price hikes. It could lead to a travel slump, as consumers wait for prices to fall again. Australia’s Qantas Airways, Scandinavia’s SAS, and Air New Zealand are three of the airlines to have already announced airfare hikes in response to the ongoing conflict in the Middle…

item.title: Zimbabwe’s Surprise Lithium Ban Scrambles Global Battery Supply Chains
item.description: This week, Zimbabwe took a historic step to protect its own value chains from external exploitation by fast-tracking a ban on raw lithium exports, effective until further notice – and the impacts have been widespread both domestically and abroad. The February 25 ban was immediate and unexpected, as were its impacts on global battery supply chains and local mining operations. Originally, the export ban was planned for January 2027, with the intent of incentivizing the local processing and refining of lithium instead of leaving value…

item.title: Trump’s Alaska Energy Revival Hits a Wall as Auction Draws Zero Bids
item.description: Since coming into power in January 2025, United States President Trump has pursued an energy policy centred around fossil fuels. As part of his plan to expand U.S. oil and gas production, Trump has made it easier to attain licences to dig for fossil fuels on federal land and has reopened several regions to exploration. One such region is Alaska, where new exploration had previously been restricted due to environmental concerns. However, during the recent sale of oil and gas leases in Alaska, there was little interest in the region, suggesting that…
